title: Anchored symplectic embeddings
speaker: Agniva Roy
abstract: Symplectic manifolds exhibit curious behaviour at the interface of rigidity and flexibility. A non-squeezing phenomenon discovered by Gromov in the 1980s was the first manifestation of this. Since then, extensive research has been carried out into when standard symplectic shapes embed inside another -- it turns out that even when volume obstructions vanish, sometimes they cannot. A mysterious connection to Markov numbers, a generalization of the Fibonacci numbers, and an infinite staircase, is exhibited in the study of embeddings of ellipsoids into balls. In other cases ingenious constructions such as folding have been invented to find embeddings. In recent work with Hutchings, Weiler, and Yao, I studied the embedding problem for four-dimensional symplectic shapes in conjunction with the question of existence of symplectic surfaces (called anchors) inside the embedding region, which has revealed some interesting results. In this talk I will survey some of the results and time permitting, discuss the ideas behind the proof and further questions.

proof_stages.label: Anchored symplectic embeddings as a strengthened fitting problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from ordinary symplectic embedding questions and defines an anchored embedding as a symplectic embedding whose complement contains a symplectic surface with prescribed transverse-knot boundary, with an n-anchored version recording the number of surface components.

proof_stages.label: Toric domains, standard anchors, and main theorems
proof_stages.summary: Convex toric domains in C^2 and their natural boundary Reeb orbits e_{1,0} and e_{0,1} are introduced, and the main results are stated: anchored embeddings force visible toric inclusions or slope/height inequalities, including the polydisk-to-ball bound c >= a+1.

proof_stages.label: Visible toric constructions and comparison with unanchored embeddings
proof_stages.summary: For the existence directions, visible cylinders or curves in the moment picture lift to symplectic anchor surfaces, while comparison with folding embeddings shows that an ordinary symplectic embedding may exist even when no such anchored surface can.

proof_stages.label: ECH and holomorphic-curve strategy for obstructions
proof_stages.summary: Toric inclusion is reduced to inequalities between Reeb-orbit actions, and the proposed mechanism is that an embedding gives an ECH cobordism map, the anchor can be deformed to a holomorphic curve, and positivity of intersections forces the needed inequalities.

proof_stages.label: ECH generators and toric combinatorics
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the ECH chain complex, grading, cobordism maps, and Hutchings's convex-integral-path model for ECH generators of convex toric domains, including how their actions and indices are read combinatorially.

proof_stages.label: Intersection-linking lemma from the anchored cylinder
proof_stages.summary: A key lemma is proved: if the ECH cobordism map sends a combinatorial generator alpha_plus to alpha_minus and an anchored holomorphic cylinder runs between e_{1,0} orbits, then positivity of intersections implies y(alpha_plus) >= y(alpha_minus).

proof_stages.label: Application to the polydisk-ball obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Applying the lemma to the ECH-index-four generator e_{1,1} in the ball leaves only the relevant polydisk generators and compares their actions, yielding the necessary inequality c >= min(1+a,2a)=1+a.

examples.note: The established ellipsoid embedding problem and its non-volume obstructions motivate asking for the stronger anchored version, but no proof step in the talk depends on the ellipsoid case itself.

examples.name: Torus-knot anchors as a future variant

examples.name: Schlenk folding embedding below the anchored bound
examples.note: Schlenk's folding embedding of a polydisk into a ball of capacity 16/5 is contrasted with the anchored obstruction requiring capacity 17/5 in the illustrated case, showing that ordinary embeddings need not carry the desired anchor surface.
examples.engagement_reason: The Schlenk folding embedding is compared numerically with the anchored bound in the toric picture, yielding the conclusion that the folding embedding into the smaller ball has no such anchored surface.

passes.reason: Schlenk’s folding embedding is used to show an ordinary symplectic embedding can exist below the anchored threshold
